heading: Move One Level Out
body: An **outer expression** is a larger formula built from a smaller part that has already been evaluated. In a truth table, once the inner part is finished, the next step is to use that finished column to compute the next larger part.

examples.text: In $\sim (P \wedge Q)$, the outer expression uses the finished column for $(P \wedge Q)$.
In $((P \vee Q) \rightarrow R)$, the conditional is outer to $(P \vee Q)$.

heading: Use The Finished Column
body: A **derived column** is a new column built from a column already completed. To evaluate an outer expression, use the truth values from the inner column directly, row by row.

examples.text: To compute $\sim (P \wedge Q)$, use the finished values of $(P \wedge Q)$.
To compute $\sim (P \wedge Q)$, ignore $(P \wedge Q)$ and start again from $P$ and $Q$ only.

heading: Negation Example
body: When the outer operator is negation, flip each truth value in the finished inner column.

examples.text: If $(P \wedge Q)$ is `T F F F`, then $\sim (P \wedge Q)$ is `F T T T`.
If $(P \wedge Q)$ is `T F F F`, then $\sim (P \wedge Q)$ is `T F F F`.

heading: Keep Row Alignment
body: **Row alignment** means each new truth value must stay in the same row as the value it comes from. Outer evaluation never changes the row order.

examples.text: If row 3 of $(P \wedge Q)$ is F, then row 3 of $\sim (P \wedge Q)$ must be T.
When building $\sim (P \wedge Q)$, values may be rearranged if the final pattern looks simpler.

heading: Do Not Skip Structure
body: A common mistake is jumping from base columns straight to the whole formula. Outer evaluation must follow the syntax one level at a time.

examples.text: In $\sim (P \wedge Q) \vee R$, compute $\sim (P \wedge Q)$ before the final disjunction.
In $\sim (P \wedge Q) \vee R$, compute the final disjunction before $\sim (P \wedge Q)$.

note: Truth-table order follows grouping, not convenience.

heading: Core Method
body: After an inner parenthesized part is complete, use that finished column to evaluate the next outer level. Work outward one operation at a time, keeping every row in place.
